questions.question: During the Lomuto partition step of quicksort with pivot value 10, the array is [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10] and the pivot is placed at index 0 first. After partitioning, which statement is guaranteed?
options: All elements before the pivot's final position are strictly less than 10
The pivot is at its final sorted position with smaller elements to the left and larger elements to the right
The left and right sub-arrays are each sorted
The array is fully sorted
questions.explanation: The partition invariant guarantees that the pivot lands at its final sorted index: everything to its left is <= pivot and everything to its right is >= pivot. The sub-arrays themselves are NOT sorted — that's what the recursive calls handle.

questions.question: Merge sort is guaranteed O(n log n) in the worst case. What is the primary cost you pay for this guarantee compared to quicksort?
options: More comparisons in the average case
O(n) additional space for the merge buffer
It cannot sort linked lists
It is not stable
questions.explanation: Merge sort's guaranteed O(n log n) comes at the cost of O(n) extra space for temporary buffers during merging. Quicksort is in-place (O(log n) stack space) but has O(n^2) worst case.

questions.question: An array has 1000 elements where 95% of the values are identical. Which sorting approach will be most efficient?
options: Standard quicksort with first-element pivot
Quicksort with 3-way partition (Dutch National Flag)
Merge sort with O(n) extra space
Insertion sort
questions.explanation: 3-way partition (Dutch National Flag) groups all equal elements in a single pass, so the recursive calls only process the ~5% distinct elements. This gives O(n) performance on inputs with many duplicates, whereas standard quicksort degrades because the pivot doesn't split the array well.

questions.question: Quickselect with random pivot selection finds the k-th smallest element in an array of n elements. What is its average-case time complexity and why?
options: O(n log n) because it must partially sort the array
O(n) because each partition step halves the search space on average, summing to n + n/2 + n/4 + ... = O(n)
O(n^2) because the worst case happens often with random pivots
O(k log n) because it performs log n partitions each touching k elements
questions.explanation: On average, a random pivot splits the array roughly in half, so we recurse into only one side. The work is n + n/2 + n/4 + ... which converges to O(n). This is the same geometric series argument that gives binary search O(log n), but applied to a linear-time partition step.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of quicksort?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n log^2 n)
questions.explanation: Quicksort degrades to O(n^2) when the pivot consistently splits the array into sizes 0 and n-1 (e.g., already-sorted input with first-element pivot). This creates n recursive calls each doing O(n) work.
